pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a person's baseline level of subjective well-being is largely fixed by temperament and genetics and does not change dramatically across life circumstances; achieving a goal does not produce the lasting happiness anticipated, because the brain returns to its dispositional baseline.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi defines adaptability as whether a person’s new behavioral baseline after recovery is higher, equal to, or lower than their old baseline. High adaptability means the hardship strengthened behavior; medium adaptability means returning exactly to the prior baseline; low adaptability means settling permanently below the prior baseline.

pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi defines tolerance as the quantity or duration of hardship a person can endure before their behavior changes from their intended baseline. High tolerance means it takes a large accumulation of adversity to change behavior; low tolerance means almost any inconvenience disrupts behavior.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi claims that the hardest part of any pursuit is not the beginning (getting started) but continuing to do unglamorous repetitive work once the initial excitement fades and rewards are not guaranteed. pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi identifies a pattern he calls playing business on defense rather than offense: owners in strong unit-economics positions spend only enough to cover fixed costs but never enough to compound growth, because they are psychologically oriented toward avoiding poverty rather than building wealth.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi argues that whatever is making a restaurant unattractive for the founder to scale as a corporate location is the same factor making it unattractive for franchisees to buy in. The two problems — slow corporate expansion and slow franchise sales — share a single root cause: insufficient per-unit profitability.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that the reason making bets is so hard psychologically is that the cost of a trade is fixed and guaranteed in the actor's mind, while the upside is uncertain and unquantified; this asymmetry in perceived certainty (not actual value) causes systematic under-betting.

The pattern suggests that in long-duration competitive markets, survival rate can matter more than growth rate.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi asserts that younger people experience the same objective magnitude of hardship as older people but suffer more from it because they lack the accumulated toolkit of survival evidence; every adult can find, upon reflection, something they survived at a younger age that was objectively harder than their current challenge.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi claims that the worst unconscious trades are the ones a person makes by default without choosing them: sacrificing youthful experiences in one's 20s while also failing to build skills or capital, receiving neither the experience nor the compound benefit.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that hardship on an entrepreneurial path is a selection-effect signal, not a warning sign: the harder a path is, the fewer people will continue on it, meaning difficulty creates a competitive moat for those who persist. Reframing hard as 'no one else will do this' flips the emotional valence from self-pity to competitive advantage.

pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi asserts that resilience is the most workable component of the four for most people who do not yet have the outcomes they want, and it is the one he would most recommend focusing on first. pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi defines tolerance as the quantity or duration of hardship a person can endure before their behavior changes from their intended baseline. High tolerance means it takes a large accumulation of adversity to change behavior; low tolerance means almost any inconvenience disrupts behavior.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi claims that all meaningful upside in life is located on the other side of uncertainty and delay, and these are the only two prices the market systematically refuses to pay. Because both uncertainty (not knowing the outcome) and delay (not knowing how long one must persist) feel unbearable to most people, they represent the primary source of arbitrage for anyone willing to bear them.

pairs.a_text: According to Alex Hormozi, the decision to extend trust should be based on two independent criteria: (1) does the other party have a track record of protecting what they have been given, and (2) does betraying you cost them more than protecting you, i.e., is burning you a bad deal for them? Only when both conditions are true does extending trust make rational sense.
